1:
The reign opens with two acts the witnesses weigh in opposite scales, and the chapter's first great division is whether Solomon's marriage to Pharaoh's daughter was defensible or a seed of ruin. The grammatical-historical commentators set the political stage. Charles Ellicott observes from the Egyptian records that Egypt was then weak and divided under the Tanite twenty-first dynasty, so that a Pharaoh who at other times might have spurned such an alliance now consented to it; and he insists that no fault is found with the match by the sacred historian, since the Egyptians were never held in the aversion reserved for the Canaanite women, and the union is nowhere tied to Solomon's later apostasy in 1 Kings 11. Keil and Delitzsch frame the whole opening as the religious consecration of a new reign. The reformed John Gill works at the identity of the king, noting that Pharaoh was a common name of the Egyptian kings and canvassing whether this one was Vaphres, as the heathen writer Eupolemus held, or Sesostris, as Calvisius thought.

The sharpest voice is the wesleyan Adam Clarke, who reads the marriage as a plain breach of law. It was a political measure to strengthen the kingdom, but God had strictly forbidden alliances with heathen women lest they turn the heart to idolatry (Exodus 34:16; Deuteronomy 7:3); and Clarke dismantles the apologists one by one — there is no evidence the princess was a proselyte, God's positive law cannot have been pleased by its own breach, and Solomon is expressly reproved for the very match in Nehemiah 13:26 and again where his declension is traced to it in 1 Kings 11:1-2. He thinks the reign began inauspiciously, and that no man ever made a worse use of great advantages.

The puritan Matthew Henry holds the middle ground and the pastoral point. Something here is unquestionably good — Solomon loved the Lord, walked in David's statutes, and gave like a king; and something is doubtful. Granting even that the bride was proselyted, the marriage was surely not advisable: he that loved the Lord should, for the Lord's sake, have fixed his love upon one of the Lord's people, for unequal matches of the sons of God with the daughters of men have often been of pernicious consequence. Yet Henry allows the kinder tradition that made these nuptials typical of the church's espousal to Christ, and leaves the reader with the character that outshines Solomon's wealth: he loved the Lord.
2:
This verse is the historian's apology for a whole nation's worship, and the witnesses read it as a considered concession rather than an approval. The grammatical-historical commentators trace the qualifying only. Keil and Delitzsch render the limiting particle as an antithesis to the strengthening of the kingdom just described: firmly as Solomon's authority was established, the people, and even Solomon himself, could as yet sacrifice to the Lord only upon the altars of the high places, because the temple was not built. Charles Ellicott presses the point that the writer speaks from the standpoint of his own later time, when after the Temple's consecration such worship was forbidden, and he carefully distinguishes two kinds of high place. The one was sheer abomination — the sacrificial groves of the Canaanite races denounced in Deuteronomy 12:2-3; the other was an unauthorised sanctuary of the true God, tolerated for a season but alien to the Mosaic law of the one chosen place.

Ellicott finds in the eventual prohibition a threefold purpose: to guard against local corruptions and any visible representation of God, to prevent the breach of national unity by rival tribal sanctuaries, and, most strikingly, to serve as a spiritual education for the worship of the invisible God without local emblems of His presence. He grants that before the Captivity there must have been some non-sacrificial places of prayer, not unlike the later synagogues, and reads Psalms 74:8 of their destruction; but these were different in idea from the high places that rivalled the Temple. He notes that even under Samuel and David sacrifice outside the tabernacle was used without scruple (1 Samuel 7:10; 1 Samuel 14:35), though it was still alien to the spirit of the Law.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke asks the plain question whether there was any sin in it, and answers historically: prophets, judges, and kings had all sacrificed on high places, and while the ark and tabernacle still stood — now at Gibeon (2 Chronicles 1:3) — sacrifice was lawful wherever they were; only after the Temple rose did it become sinful elsewhere. The reformed John Gill adds the concrete picture, locating this worship on the tops of houses, on hills and mountains, and chiefly at the great high place in Gibeon where the tabernacle stood. The company agrees: the practice was a weakness God tolerated for a time, not a pattern He approved, and the verse quietly prepares for the day when the one house in Jerusalem would gather all this scattered devotion to a single altar.
3:
The verse holds a commendation and a caution in a single breath — Solomon loved the Lord, only he sacrificed and burnt incense in high places — and the witnesses refuse to let either half cancel the other. The reformed John Gill fastens first on the love, glossing it with the Targum as love for the worship of the Lord, the affection that shows itself in obedience and communion. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch hold the tension exactly: even Solomon, though he loved the Lord and walked in the statutes of his father David — that is, in the commandments of the Lord as written in the law of Moses (1 Kings 2:3) — still sacrificed upon the high places.

Keil explains why the practice was, for the moment, all but unavoidable. Before the building of the temple, and especially since the tabernacle had lost its old significance as the central seat of God's gracious presence through the removal of the ark of the covenant, worship at the high places could hardly be escaped. Yet he will not soften what followed: even afterward it continued as a forbidden cultus, one that could not be thoroughly rooted out by the most righteous of the later kings (2 Kings 12:3; 2 Kings 14:4; 2 Kings 15:4). The only, then, is not a small word; it marks the single shadow across a bright confession, the point at which a genuinely godly king came short of his father, who had kept his heart fixed on the ark.

The witnesses draw from this the lesson that real love for God may coexist with real irregularity, and that grace is patient with the second where the first is sincere. Solomon's heart was right, and God would presently meet him with extraordinary favour at one of these very high places; but the historian's qualifying clause stands as a standing reminder that zeal in worship is no substitute for worship ordered by God's own rule. The love is owned and praised; the irregularity is named and not excused; and the verse leaves the reader looking, as the chapter itself will, toward the day when the scattered altars would yield to the one house where God had chosen to set His name. To love the Lord is the saint's truest character, and to walk in His statutes is love's proper proof, so that where the two are found together the shadow of the high places is felt the more keenly.
4:
The king goes to Gibeon, the great high place, and offers a thousand burnt offerings upon its altar; the witnesses attend to the place, the altar, and the royal scale of the sacrifice. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the ground precisely. Keil and Delitzsch explain that the great or principal bamah was at Gibeon because the Mosaic tabernacle stood there (2 Chronicles 1:3), and it is called the great high place not for its height but because the ark of the covenant, with which Jehovah had bound up His gracious presence, was no longer with it; the thousand offerings were made upon the brazen altar of burnt-offering that belonged to the tabernacle (2 Chronicles 1:6), and, as the Chronicler adds, the representatives of the whole nation shared in the festival (2 Chronicles 1:2). Charles Ellicott reads the name itself as "belonging to a hill," places the town in the land of Benjamin on the central plateau, and recalls that the old Gibeonites had been made hewers of wood and drawers of water for this very sanctuary (Joshua 9:23), so that it was naturally the great high place.

The reformed John Gill supplies the plain geography, setting Gibeon some four or five miles from Jerusalem, near enough that the young king could bring the nation with him to worship. Between them the commentators let the sheer magnitude of the offering register: a thousand victims upon one altar is the gift of a king who, in Matthew Henry's phrase from the previous verses, offered like a king, in some proportion to his great wealth.

The witnesses find in the scene both the strength and the flaw of Solomon's early piety. The strength is the lavish, unstinting devotion of a man who would not appear before the Lord empty or grudging; where God had sown so plentifully, Solomon reaped and returned accordingly. The flaw is the one the chapter has just named — that all this was offered at a high place rather than before the ark. Yet God, who looks first at the heart, was about to honour this very sacrifice with the night-vision that follows, teaching that He meets the sincere worshipper even where the worshipper's understanding is still imperfect, and drawing him on toward the fuller obedience the Temple would one day require. The thousand offerings thus stand as the fitting prelude to the dream, the outpouring of a grateful king answered by the condescension of a gracious God.
5:
God appears to Solomon in a dream and says, Ask what I shall give thee; the witnesses dwell on the nature of the dream, the circumstances of the visit, and the astonishing freedom of the offer. The reformed John Gill insists this was no common natural dream but an extraordinary, supernatural, prophetic one — a night-vision in which Solomon had the full use of his reasoning powers and was in a spiritual frame, so that God really did appear to him while he slept. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch tie the revelation to the sacrifice: the one thing wanting at Gibeon, the ark with its gracious presence, was supplied by this direct word, and the question What shall I give thee? answers the very object for which Solomon had come, to seek God's help for his reign. Charles Ellicott reads the mode more coolly, noting that the vision by dream is a lower type of revelation, given mostly to those outside the covenant — Abimelech, Laban, Pharaoh, the Midianite, Nebuchadnezzar — or to the early stages of grace in Abraham, Jacob, and Joseph, and he takes it as a first hint of some temporary abeyance of the prophetic office.

The puritan Matthew Henry lingers over the circumstances as tokens of grace. The place was Gibeon, the great high place, where the nearer worship came to the rule the more it might expect the presence of God. The time was by night, after the day's great sacrifice, for the more we abound in God's work the more comfort we may look for in Him, and His kindest visits are often in the night (Psalms 17:3). The manner was a dream, the senses locked so that God's access to the mind might be freer, the way He often spoke to His prophets (Numbers 12:6; Job 33:15). And the offer itself is pure condescension, God putting honour on the prayer of faith much as He does for us in the gospel (John 16:23; 1 John 5:14), while Solomon, unlike Ahaz who would not ask (Isaiah 7:12), lays hold of it at once.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke alone canvasses a natural explanation, granting that the dream came after Solomon's sacrifice and likely his earnest prayer (2 Chronicles 1:7), so that the images of the day might recur by night; yet he concludes it was supernatural, a dream by which the very wisdom was communicated. On the substance the voices agree: whatever the mechanism, the grace was real, and God stooped to a sleeping king to make him a waking gift.
6:
Solomon's prayer begins not with his own need but with God's mercy to David, and the witnesses read the opening as a model of covenant gratitude and humility. The reformed John Gill settles the plain question first: Solomon really said these words in his dream, not merely dreamt that he said them. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch show the grammar of devotion, the whole petition hung as an apodosis upon the acknowledgment that God had shown great mercy to David and kept it for him in giving a son to sit on his throne, as it is this day.

Charles Ellicott draws out the character of the wisdom Solomon is reaching toward. It is the wisdom of the ruler, involving elements both moral and intellectual — the wisdom to discern and do true justice between man and man. He notes that the king calls himself a little child, his age variously reckoned from twelve to twenty, and that he trembles at the responsibility of ruling so great a people. And Ellicott marks what is most characteristic of Old Testament godliness here: Solomon looks for wisdom not as the mere fruit of human teaching and experience but as an inspiration of God, and prays for it accordingly, in a prayer of singular beauty and humility, pleading simply God's promise to his father and its fulfilment in his own accession.

The puritan Matthew Henry presses the filial piety of the words. Solomon speaks honourably of his father's uprightness, drawing a veil over his faults, and more honourably still of God's goodness to David — the mercy shown him while he lived and the kindness kept for his family when he was gone. Children should give God thanks for His mercies to their parents, for the sure mercies of David are doubly sweet when we trace them transmitted to us through the hands of those who went before; and the way to see the entail perpetuated is to bless God that it has hitherto been preserved. The witnesses land together on the same note: true prayer begins in the memory of grace already given, and true greatness begins where a king counts himself a little child and asks his crown's chief gift from heaven. Solomon rests his whole plea on what God had freely done for David, so that the mercy of one generation becomes the confidence of the next.
7:
"Thou hast made thy servant king instead of David my father: and I am but a little child; I know not how to go out or come in." The witnesses hear in the confession the disarming humility of a man who feels the weight of his office. The reformed John Gill fixes on the first clause, that Solomon was made king in his father's stead by the appointment of God and through His overruling providence — set on the throne notwithstanding the attempts that had been made to prevent it, so that he ascribes his accession wholly to God and not to his own hand. The wesleyan Adam Clarke unfolds the vivid figure of the second clause: Solomon is like an infant learning to walk alone, who can neither go out nor come in without help, a childlike helplessness confessed by the wisest of kings at the very threshold of his reign.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the theology of the plea. Those who are employed in public stations ought to be deeply sensible of the weight and importance of their work and of their own insufficiency for it, and it is precisely such men who are qualified to receive divine instruction. He sets beside Solomon's who is able to judge this thy so great a people? the apostle's kindred cry, who is sufficient for these things? (2 Corinthians 2:16), and recalls that even Absalom, reckoned a wise man, trembled at the like undertaking. The more knowing and considerate men are, the better acquainted they are with their own weakness, and the more jealous of themselves.

The little child is not false modesty but true self-knowledge, and the witnesses make it the hinge of the whole prayer. A man who thought himself equal to Israel's throne would never have asked for wisdom; it is because Solomon knows he cannot go out or come in of himself that he casts his kingship back upon the God who gave it. His father David had called him a wise man (1 Kings 2:9), yet Solomon in the presence of God counts himself a child in understanding — and this lowliness is the very soil in which the promised wisdom will grow. The verse teaches that the beginning of fitness for great trust is the honest owning of unfitness, and that the ruler most likely to be taught of God is the one who most feels he has everything to learn.
8:
"And thy servant is in the midst of thy people which thou hast chosen, a great people that cannot be numbered nor counted for multitude." With only one named voice on the verse, the witnesses draw its weight from the two words that carry the plea — chosen and great. The reformed John Gill unfolds the first. This is God's peculiar people, chosen to be His special and treasured possession above all the peoples of the earth; and Solomon's being in the midst of them is not to be taken merely of place — though Jerusalem did lie in the heart of the land — but of the exercise of his office, set over them and among them, charged with their care and oversight.

The force of the argument lies in laying these two facts side by side. The people are not Solomon's but God's, chosen by Him; therefore they must be ruled for Him and answered for to Him, and the burden of governing them well presses the harder because the honour of their Owner is bound up in their government. And they are a multitude beyond numbering — the very language of the ancient promise, when God told Abraham his seed should be as the stars of heaven and the sand of the sea for multitude (Genesis 22:17). Solomon stands, as it were, inside the fulfilment of that word, a single inexperienced man set to shepherd a nation grown past counting; and the sheer disproportion between the shepherd and the flock is itself the reason for his prayer.

The witnesses find here the ground of Solomon's request in the previous and following verses. Precisely because the people are chosen and countless, no ordinary prudence will suffice; a king who is but a little child cannot manage such a charge without a gift from heaven. The election that makes Israel precious makes the responsibility of ruling them awful, and the multitude that fulfils God's promise to the fathers is the multitude Solomon must judge. So the verse turns the greatness of the trust into the measure of the need: the larger the flock and the higher its Owner's claim, the more the shepherd must lean upon the God who gave him both the people and the throne. It is a plea that honours God twice over, owning both His choice of the nation and His right to be served in its rule.
9:
The petition itself is now spoken: Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart to judge thy people, that I may discern between good and bad. The witnesses agree that the request is as remarkable for what it omits as for what it asks. The reformed John Gill defines the gift narrowly and rightly: not an understanding of things spiritual, nor of things natural, though both were afterward given him, but of things political — the discernment needful for civil government, that he might rule the people of Israel in the best manner. The wesleyan Adam Clarke says the same in a phrase: Solomon did not ask wisdom in general but the true science of government; this he sought, and this he obtained.

The grammatical-historical reading lingers over the Hebrew, an obedient or hearing heart, the faculty by which a judge weighs the parties before him and separates the true from the false. To discern between good and bad is the whole art of the bench, and Solomon asks for it as the one thing his office cannot do without. The puritan Matthew Henry presses the pattern of the prayer for every believer's imitation. An understanding heart is God's gift (Proverbs 2:6); we must pray for it (James 1:5), and pray for it with application to our particular calling and the various occasions we have for it, as Solomon does — not to please curiosity or puzzle his neighbours, but to judge God's people. That is the best knowledge which is serviceable to us in doing our duty, enabling us to tell good from bad, right from wrong, truth from falsehood, so that we are not imposed upon by false colours in judging either others' actions or our own.

The witnesses land on the wisdom of the choice. Solomon might have asked long life, riches, or the ruin of his enemies; he asked instead for the very thing his charge required, and asked it of God rather than of experience. Henry notes that he calls himself God's servant, pleased with that relation and pleading it — as his father had pleaded, I am thy servant, give me understanding (Psalms 119:125). The prayer models the ordering of a right heart: to want most the grace that fits us for our duty, and to seek it from the hand that alone can bestow it.
10:
"And the speech pleased the Lord, that Solomon had asked this thing." The witnesses read the divine approval as a verdict on the whole cast of Solomon's desire. The reformed John Gill explains what it was that pleased God: Solomon had respect not to his own private benefit and advantage but to the good of the people he governed and the honour and glory of God, who had set him over them as His vicegerent. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch unfold the ground of the pleasure from God's own words that follow — because Solomon had not asked for himself long life, nor riches, nor the life of his enemies, all of them good things which the world seeks as its greatest prize, but had asked understanding to hear judgment, the discernment on which a conscientious administration of justice rests. Keil adds that the promise none like thee is to be taken quite universally, not merely of the kings of Israel but of all mankind (compare 1 Kings 4:29).

The puritan Matthew Henry reaches behind the pleasing prayer to its author. God is well pleased with His own work in His people, the desires of His own kindling, the prayers of His Spirit's inditing; so that Solomon's acceptable request is itself a token of grace already at work in him. By this choice Solomon made it appear that he desired to be good more than great, and to serve God's honour more than to advance his own; and those are ever accepted of God who prefer spiritual blessings to temporal, and who are more solicitous to be found in the way of their duty than in the way to preferment.

The witnesses draw the encouragement plain. Prayer that seeks first the interest of God and the good of others is prayer God delights to hear; the petition that pleased Him was the unselfish one. And there is more here than a single approval, for the same verses that record God's pleasure record that it prevailed for far more than Solomon asked — a truth the following verses will unfold. The lesson stands: ask for the right thing, the thing that serves God and His people, and you ask what God is glad to give, and glad to give beyond the measure of the asking. The speech that pleased the Lord is the pattern of every prayer that puts His glory before its own comfort.
11:
God rehearses Solomon's request by naming all he did not ask, and the witnesses find in that list the true measure of the man's heart. The grammatical-historical commentators set the verse under the great gospel maxim. Keil and Delitzsch read it by the promise that to him who seeks first the kingdom of God and His righteousness all other things shall be added (Matthew 6:33), so that the very blessings Solomon passed over — long life, riches, the life of his enemies — are the things God now freely lays upon him. Charles Ellicott makes the same connection and adds the sober qualification: all these secondary blessings are good just so far as they conduce to the supreme good, the growth of a human nature by the knowledge of God and faithful work on earth; so long as Solomon used them in subordination to true wisdom they were a blessing, but when he made them idols they became a curse. The reformed John Gill notes only that God said this to Solomon while he was yet in the dream, keeping the whole exchange within the one revelation.

The witnesses press the discernment of the choice. Long life, wealth, and victory over enemies are precisely the things ambition covets and the things a young king might most naturally have grasped at; that Solomon left them all unasked, seeking only the wisdom to govern well, is what God singles out for commendation. The verse turns the omissions into the argument: it is not merely that he asked a good thing, but that he refused the many good things a lesser soul would have seized, and reached past them to the one gift that fitted him for his duty and God's glory.

And so the verse holds out a rule of desire. God is not displeased by riches or length of days in themselves — He is about to bestow them — but He is pleased when they are not the heart's first cry. Ellicott's caution keeps the promise honest: the same gifts that crown a wise and God-fearing king can ruin him the moment they become his idols, as Solomon's own later history would prove. To seek first the kingdom is to have the rest added; to seek first the rest is to endanger the soul. The one thing needful, sought before all else, draws the many things after it, and holds them in their proper place beneath it.
12:
"Behold, I have done according to thy words: lo, I have given thee a wise and an understanding heart; so that there was none like thee before thee, neither after thee shall any arise like unto thee." Here the witnesses divide over whether the gift is bestowed absolutely or held under condition. The reformed John Gill reads the grant as already accomplished: God not only promised the wisdom but had done it, or at least begun to do it, so that the answer runs ahead of the asking. The grammatical-historical reading takes the superlative none like thee in the widest sense — no ruler in Israel or among the nations, before or after, would match this endowment.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke is the dissenting and searching voice. He grants that God gave Solomon a capacious mind, capable of knowing much, and bids him make proper use of his powers under the direction of God's Spirit; but he presses a pointed query — was not all this conditional upon Solomon's walking in God's ways and keeping His commandments, as the next verse requires? Did Solomon in the end receive the whole of what was here offered? Clarke answers with a lament: his unfaithfulness prevented the full purpose of God, so that the man promised to be wisest of all became, in Clarke's blunt phrase, more brutish than any man, losing the knowledge of his Creator and worshipping the abominations of Moab and Zidon. No character in the sacred writings, he says, disappoints us more than Solomon; he received much, but would have received far more had he been faithful to the grace given.

The difference is real but bounded. All agree that a wisdom unequalled among men was truly and freely given, and that it was displayed at once in the judgment that follows and in the fame it won him. The dispute is over its issue: whether the promise was a simple bestowal that Solomon possessed to the end, or a gift whose fuller fruit was forfeited by his later apostasy. Gill and the grammatical-historical voices dwell on what was given; Clarke dwells on what was squandered. Between them the verse stands as both a wonder and a warning — the highest natural endowment God ever laid on a king, and the sobering proof that even such a gift, unguarded by obedience, cannot secure the man who holds it. Clarke's closing note fixes the plain sense of the superlative: none like thee before thee means no king, whether in Israel or among the nations.
13:
"And I have also given thee that which thou hast not asked, both riches and honour: so that there shall not be any among the kings like unto thee all thy days." The verse records the overflow of God's generosity, and the witnesses read it as the visible proof of the gospel principle that the previous verses had named. The reformed John Gill keeps the tense exact: God had intended to give these things, now promised them, and was about to bestow them, so that the unasked riches and honour follow the asked-for wisdom as surely as the shadow follows the substance.

The grammatical-historical commentators had already fixed the principle in the surrounding verses — that to him who seeks first the kingdom all these things shall be added (Matthew 6:33) — and this verse is its literal enactment. God gives what Solomon sought and then heaps on what he did not seek, riches and honour such as no king of the earth had known before him. The added blessings are not wrung from a reluctant hand; they are pressed upon a king who had the grace not to ask them, and their very abundance is a comment on the wisdom of his choice. Had Solomon begged for wealth, he might have had wealth and missed the wisdom; because he begged for wisdom, he had the wisdom and the wealth besides.

The witnesses draw the twofold lesson the verse teaches. First, the sovereign liberality of God, who delights to do more than we ask or think, and who joins to His chosen gift a train of mercies the asker never named. Second, the safe order of a well-governed desire: seek the spiritual first and the temporal is added; seek the temporal first and you may forfeit both. Solomon's throne would soon shine with a splendour unequalled among the kingdoms of the earth, the fruit of David's long labours brought to sudden flower; and every ingot of it preached that God is no man's debtor, and that the surest way to the lesser goods is to have wanted the greater more. The unasked gift is the measure of the Giver, who crowns a right prayer not only with its request but with a bounty beyond it. What Solomon left out of his petition, God would not leave out of His answer.
14:
The promise closes with a single conditioned clause: if thou wilt walk in my ways, to keep my statutes and my commandments, as thy father David did walk, then I will lengthen thy days. The witnesses notice at once that only this last gift is hung upon a condition, and that this was the one gift Solomon did not finally receive. The reformed John Gill glosses the way plainly — the ways of God prescribed and directed in His word — so that long life is tied to a persevering obedience patterned on David's.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is exact and sobering. In this whole promise only one point, length of days, is conditional, and it was not fulfilled: for though Solomon's age at death is nowhere given, his reign of forty years can scarcely have let it exceed sixty, whatever Josephus may say in extending the reign to eighty. The rest of the promise, Ellicott notes, received an extraordinary fulfilment — the greatness of Solomon's kingdom standing out in its sudden and unique development, the fruit of David's long career of conquest, when for the first and last time the monarchy took on something of the character of an empire, unequalled in peaceful wealth and splendour, yet destined to wither at Solomon's death. Keil and Delitzsch read the same terms and pass straight to the waking, when Solomon rose and knew it had been a dream from God.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the moral out of the contrast. God promised riches and honour absolutely, but long life upon condition; Solomon failed in the condition, and therefore, though he had riches and honour, he did not live to enjoy them so long as in the course of nature he might have done. Henry sets the whole under the figure of wisdom's two hands: length of days is her right-hand blessing, typical of eternal life, while riches and honour are in her left (Proverbs 3:16). The witnesses agree without dispute: the conditional word was a real word, and its non-fulfilment is written into Solomon's history as the plainest of warnings. The unconditional gifts stood; the conditioned gift was lost; and the difference between them is measured by a life that did not keep the ways of David to the end. Obedience, the verse quietly teaches, is not the price of God's free mercies but the appointed path along which some of His best blessings are received.
15:
Solomon awakes, returns to Jerusalem, stands before the ark, offers burnt and peace offerings, and makes a feast; the witnesses read the waking and the worship as the seal of the vision. The reformed John Gill guards the phrase it was a dream against misreading — not that it was nothing but a dream, a vain and empty one, but a divine and supernatural dream of prophecy, whose truth Solomon perceived the instant he woke, finding himself possessed of a wisdom he had never had before, which occasioned the thanksgiving that follows.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the place of the thank-offering. Charles Ellicott notes that Solomon stood before the ark of the covenant in its tabernacle on Mount Zion, now a second and probably still more sacred place of worship, where the great sacrifice — distinctly a thank-offering, followed as usual by a sacred feast — was naturally repeated. Keil and Delitzsch describe the same: on returning Solomon thanked the Lord afresh for the promise, offering burnt and peace offerings before the ark upon Zion and preparing a sacrificial meal of the shelamim for all his court-servants.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the richest lesson from the change of place. Solomon awoke, we may suppose, in a transport of joy, his sleep sweet to him (Jeremiah 31:26); and being satisfied of God's favour, he began to think what he should render to the Lord. He had prayed at the high place at Gibeon and there God had graciously met him, but now he comes to Jerusalem to give thanks before the ark of the covenant — as if blaming himself that he had not prayed there, the ark being the token of God's presence, and wondering that God had met him anywhere else. God's passing by our mistakes, Henry observes, should persuade us to amend them; and there Solomon offered a great sacrifice and made a great feast, that those about him might rejoice with him in the grace of God. So the witnesses hold together a gentle nuance: where Ellicott sees simply a second sacred place, Henry hears a quiet self-correction, the king drawn from the tolerated high place back to the ark where God had chosen to record His name. Either way the vision issues in worship, and the gift of wisdom in a grateful and public thanksgiving.
16:
The chapter turns from promise to proof, and two women come to the king; the witnesses divide first over who these women were and then unite in reading the scene as the God-given wisdom put to the test. The reformed John Gill sees a providence in the timing — an immediate instance, the very day after the vision, to display the wisdom Solomon had received. On their character he is of two minds: the Targum makes them victuallers or innkeepers, and Ben Gersom sellers of food like Rahab, which their care for the child would favour, since common prostitutes seldom bear or cherish children; yet their being alone, without husbands, and their impudent, brawling contention give great suspicion of the harlots the version names. The wesleyan Adam Clarke presses the milder rendering hard: the Hebrew zonoth is better given, with the Chaldee, as tavern-keepers; had they been harlots they would scarcely have dared appear before Solomon, nor would such women commonly have children, and their husbands were likely away at their occupations.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lifts the eye from the women to the judgment. This celebrated judgment of Solomon is an instance of intuitive sagacity, cutting the Gordian knot of a hopeless difficulty by an appeal to maternal instinct — an appeal that might, of course, have failed, but was under the circumstances the only appeal possible. It is in knowing how to risk failure rather than be reduced to impotence, and how to go straight to the heart of a difficulty when the slow approaches of ordinary method are shut off, that men recognise a touch of genius and Scripture recognises the wisdom of God. Keil and Delitzsch lay out the bare facts as the case will unfold them.

The puritan Matthew Henry sets the human scene. The cause is opened not by lawyers but by the parties themselves, though women, which made it the easier for so piercing an eye to discern right from wrong by their own showing; it had likely been heard in the inferior courts and found too hard, that Solomon's wisdom in deciding it might be the more remarked. And Henry draws a tender observation from the tale itself — how much better it was then with children born out of wedlock than commonly since, for these harlots loved their infants and were loath to part with them, whereas in an evil day men should be without natural affection (2 Timothy 3:3). So the witnesses gather the threads: whatever the women's trade, the case is real, the difficulty genuine, and the stage set for the wisdom of God to be shown in the sight of all the people.
17:
The first woman opens her plea: O my lord, I and this woman dwell in one house; and I was delivered of a child with her in the house. The witnesses read the statement as the careful setting of a case that will turn out to have no witnesses at all. The reformed John Gill marks her simply as the plaintiff, the one who brings the complaint before the king. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay the facts in order as she tells them and as the following verses will repeat: two women living together in one house, each delivered of a child within three days of the other, no stranger with them, and then in the night the death of the one child and the secret exchange that has brought them before the throne.

The pathos of the opening lies in its very circumstances. These are two poor women, without servant or nurse, without husband named or friend to attend them, sharing a single roof and passing through the perils of childbirth alone; and it is out of that isolation that the whole difficulty grows. For the woman's careful there was no stranger with us in the house is not incidental — it is the heart of the legal problem. No third party saw what happened; there is no one to corroborate either mother's word; and so the case reduces to oath against oath, one woman's bare assertion set flat against the other's denial.

The witnesses feel the weight of this. The plaintiff tells a coherent and moving story — the shared house, the near-simultaneous births, the night, the substitution she claims to have discovered by the morning light — but a coherent story is not proof, and she can offer nothing beyond her own account. Solomon must weigh a narrative that cannot be verified, spoken by a woman who cannot be shown to be more honest than her rival. Here the difficulty of the whole episode is laid bare in the plaintiff's first words: the truth is real, a true mother and a true child stand somewhere in the room, but the ordinary machinery of evidence has nothing to lay hold of. The plea is an appeal to a justice that can see further than testimony reaches, and it prepares the reader to watch how a wisdom given from heaven will find the truth that human witnesses could not supply. What she cannot prove, the king must somehow discern.
18:
"And it came to pass the third day after that I was delivered, that this woman was delivered also: and we were together; there was no stranger with us in the house, save we two in the house." The witnesses read the verse as the plaintiff pressing home the one fact that makes her case both credible and unprovable. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that she speaks of the third day after she herself had been delivered of a child, marking the short interval between the two births.

That interval is not idle detail. The two women were brought to bed within three days of one another, so that both infants were newborn and nearly of an age — near enough that, once the living and the dead were exchanged in the dark, no glance at the children could settle which belonged to which. And the doubled insistence that they were alone — we were together; there was no stranger with us in the house, save we two — fastens the difficulty into place. There was no midwife, no servant, no neighbour lodged with them; nothing passed between the two mothers that a third eye witnessed.

The puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the whole episode, names exactly this as the knot of it: the difficulty of the case was that there was no evidence on either side. The neighbours, though some may have been present at the birth, had not so noted the children as to be able to distinguish them; and to put the parties to torture would have favoured not the woman who had justice on her side but the one who was most hardy, so that little stress could be laid on evidence so extorted. Judges and juries, Henry observes, have need of wisdom to find out truth when it thus lies hid. The witnesses therefore let the verse do its quiet work: by establishing the closeness of the births and the absence of any witness, the plaintiff has, without meaning to, defined the impossibility that Solomon must overcome. The facts are simple; the proof is nowhere; and the case is being built, detail by detail, into the very shape that only a divinely given discernment could untie. Every particular she offers to strengthen her claim strengthens equally the difficulty of judging it.
19:
"And this woman's child died in the night, because she overlaid it." The witnesses read the verse as the hinge of the whole complaint — the accident in the dark from which the fraud is said to have sprung. The reformed John Gill observes that it is not certain whether this was the very night the child was born or some night after, but the fact stands: the woman, sleeping, lay upon her infant, and it died beneath her.

The plaintiff's charge is precise. She does not accuse her rival of murder but of a fatal carelessness — the child smothered in sleep, overlaid by the mother's own body — a danger only too familiar where a newborn shares its mother's bed. It is the kind of loss that needs no malice to explain it, and the very ordinariness of the tragedy lends the story its ring of truth. Out of this nocturnal accident, the plaintiff will claim, grew the desperate exchange of the living child for the dead.

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the verse into a meditation on the fragility of infant life. Little children, he reflects, are the occasion of much anxiety, their lives uncertain and hourly exposed to danger; the age of infancy is the valley of the shadow of death, and the lamp of life, when first lighted, is easily blown out. It is a wonder of mercy, he adds, that so few perish amid the perils of nursing. The witnesses let the dead child stand as a sombre reminder of that frailty, and as the necessary background to what follows. For the death itself is not in dispute; a child has died in the house, and one of these two women is its mother. The whole legal tangle turns on which of them woke to find her arms empty — and whether the survivor in the living child's cradle is telling the truth or wearing another woman's motherhood. The verse states the loss without adornment, and leaves the reader, like the court, waiting to see how the living and the dead will be sorted to their rightful mothers. A single careless night has set the whole difficulty in motion.
20:
"And she arose at midnight, and took my son from beside me, while thine handmaid slept, and laid it in her bosom, and laid her dead child in my bosom." This is the heart of the accusation, and the witnesses read it as the plaintiff's account of a cold and calculated exchange. The reformed John Gill supplies the motive she imputes: perceiving what she had done, that she had overlaid her own child and it was dead, the other woman — whether through fear of the punishment such negligence might bring, or through the disgrace of it — took no more care for her dead infant but resorted to this stratagem, stealing the living child and leaving the dead one in its place.

The plaintiff's charge is exact in its cruelty. It was done at midnight, in the depth of sleep; the living son was lifted from beside his mother and laid in the guilty woman's bosom, and the little corpse was laid in the true mother's arms, so that she should wake to a dead child not her own. Every clause deepens the picture of deliberate deceit: the hour chosen for its darkness, the mother's sleep exploited, the substitution made so quietly that the victim never stirred.

Yet the witnesses feel the weight of the difficulty even here, for this is still only the plaintiff's word. She was, on her own telling, asleep while it happened; she saw nothing, and can prove nothing. What she offers is a reconstruction — a plausible, even compelling account of how the dead child came to lie in her arms and the living in her rival's — but the other woman, as the next verses show, tells the story the other way about, and there is no third voice to decide between them. The very vividness of the plaintiff's narrative is also its weakness: it is precisely the tale a bereaved mother would tell to reclaim a child, and precisely the tale a thief might tell to keep one. So the exchange at midnight, real as it was for one of them, remains for the court an unwitnessed act in the dark, the crux of a case that human testimony cannot resolve and that waits for a wisdom able to read the hearts the night concealed. What was done while she slept she can only allege, not establish.
21:
"And when I rose in the morning to give my child suck, behold, it was dead: but when I had considered it in the morning, behold, it was not my son, which I did bear." The witnesses read the verse as the plaintiff's account of a mother's dawning horror and the discovery on which her whole claim rests. The reformed John Gill notes the homely detail that she rose in the morning to give her child suck, as she was used to do — a mother's ordinary act turned, in an instant, into the moment of grief and suspicion.

The verse moves in two stages, and the witnesses mark both. First the shock: she took the child to her breast and found it dead. Then the closer look — when I had considered it in the morning — by the light of day she studied the little face and knew that this was not the son she had borne. A mother's eye, that had nursed and known her own infant, could tell the difference the darkness had hidden; and so her cry is not merely that her child is dead but that this dead child is not hers at all. The dead one in her arms is another woman's; her own living son is elsewhere.

The witnesses let the pathos and the problem stand together. The claim is powerful precisely because it appeals to that intimate maternal recognition which no stranger could counterfeit — she says she knows her own child, and knew at once that this was not he. Yet it is still recognition asserted, not recognition proved; the rival mother could make, and does make, the very same claim in reverse. So the plaintiff's testimony completes her side of an unbreakable deadlock: a coherent, moving, and entirely unverifiable account, ending in a discovery that only she witnessed and only she can vouch for. Everything now waits on the king. The case has been fully stated from one side, and stated well; but a well-told grief is not evidence, and the truth of the matter — which woman bore the living child — still lies hidden behind two contradictory motherhoods, to be brought to light not by argument but by the discernment God had given. Her certainty is real, but it is a certainty the court cannot yet share.
22:
"And the other woman said, Nay; but the living is my son, and the dead is thy son. And this said, No; but the dead is thy son, and the living is my son." The witnesses read the verse as the moment the case locks into perfect deadlock. The reformed John Gill identifies the second speaker as the defendant, who meets the plaintiff's whole narrative with a flat denial and the counter-claim that reverses every term of it.

The sentence is built to sound like an echo turned inside out — each woman affirming exactly what the other denies, the living child mine and the dead child yours, until the words themselves circle without end. There is no new fact here, no fresh evidence; there is only assertion against assertion, and the reader hears the two voices collide and cancel. This they spake before the king, and the very form of the verse, with its answering claims, conveys the hopelessness of settling the matter by their words.

The puritan Matthew Henry catches the intensity of it: both mothers are vehement in their claim and peremptory in their asseverations — it is mine, says one; nay, it is mine, says the other. Neither will own the dead child, though it would be cheaper to bury that than to maintain the living; but it is the living one they strive for, the living child being the parent's joy because it is their hope. And the difficulty, Henry repeats, is that there was no evidence on either side to break the tie. The witnesses therefore let this verse stand as the pivot of the chapter. Everything up to it has narrowed the truth to a point that testimony cannot reach: two women, one house, no witness, and now two irreconcilable claims spoken to the king's face. The ordinary resources of justice are exhausted; a lesser judge might cast lots, or side with the bolder voice, or dismiss the suit unresolved. It is into exactly this impasse that Solomon's God-given wisdom will step, and the deadlock of the two voices is the dark background against which that wisdom will shine. Where the words of the parties fail, the wisdom of the king must speak.
23:
"Then said the king, The one saith, This is my son that liveth, and thy son is the dead: and the other saith, Nay; but thy son is the dead, and my son is the living." The witnesses read Solomon's words as the judicial summing-up that fixes the impasse before he resolves it. The reformed John Gill explains that the king, acting as judge, here sums up what had been said on both sides — assertions only, without proof, the one affirming precisely what the other denied, and the other denying precisely what the other affirmed.

The value of the summing-up is that it states the problem exactly. Solomon does not pretend to more than the case affords; he lays the two claims side by side in their bare contradiction, showing the court and the parties that nothing distinguishes them. Both women say the same words with the names reversed; neither can add a witness or a token; and the king, by rehearsing the deadlock aloud, makes plain that on the evidence presented the matter simply cannot be decided. This candour is itself a mark of wisdom: he will not be hurried into a verdict the facts do not warrant.

The puritan Matthew Henry pictures the whole court held in suspense at this point, in expectation of what course Solomon's wisdom will take to find out the truth. One onlooker knows not what to say to it; another, perhaps, would settle it by lot. But Solomon has weighed the case and seen that the ordinary paths are closed — there is no proof to sift, no witness to examine, no external mark to appeal to. The witnesses let the verse stand as the pause before the stroke. By naming the equal and opposite claims, the king has cleared away every false hope of an easy answer and set the stage for the extraordinary expedient that follows. The summing-up is the last word of ordinary justice in this cause; what comes next will be the wisdom of God, going by a path no rule of court could have prescribed, to draw from the hidden hearts of the two women the evidence their lips would never give. Having stated the deadlock plainly, Solomon is ready to break it.
24:
"And the king said, Bring me a sword. And they brought a sword before the king." The witnesses read the command as the startling turn on which the whole judgment hangs. The reformed John Gill observes that the design of it might not at first appear to the court, so that the order would seem strange and be greatly wondered at — what could the king mean by calling for a sword in a dispute over a living infant?

The very abruptness is deliberate. Having shown that no proof could settle the case, Solomon does not reason further but acts, and acts in a way calculated to shock. A sword in a nursery quarrel is an outrage to every expectation; the onlookers, and the two women above all, are given no time to compose themselves or calculate a response. That is the point of it. The command is not what it seems, but it must seem in deadly earnest if it is to work, and so Solomon utters it with all the gravity of a real sentence.

The puritan Matthew Henry lets us feel the consternation. Solomon calls for a sword and gives orders to divide the living child; and to the sages of the law this seemed a ridiculous decision, a brutal cutting of the knot he could not untie — is this the wisdom of Solomon? they must have thought, little dreaming what he aimed at. Henry recalls that the hearts of such kings are unsearchable (Proverbs 25:3), and that though there was an old law for dividing a living ox and a dead one between disputing owners (Exodus 21:35), that statute did not reach this case at all. The witnesses therefore hold the verse in its suspense. The sword is brought; the command hangs in the air; the court is aghast, and even the reader who knows the outcome feels the edge of the moment. What looks like the failure of justice — a judge reduced to butchery — is in truth its most cunning instrument, a trap baited with terror to catch the truth that words could not. The next verses will spring it; here the wisdom of God wears, for an instant, the mask of folly, and the whole assembly waits to see whether the king has lost his reason or found the one road to the truth. The sword that seems to end the inquiry is the very means of resolving it.
25:
"And the king said, Divide the living child in two, and give half to the one, and half to the other." The witnesses read the sentence as the deliberate stroke by which Solomon forces the hidden truth into the open, and they divide over whether the wisdom it displays is natural sagacity or a gift from heaven. The reformed John Gill notes only that the king spoke this to one of his officers, the order given as though it were to be carried out on the spot.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke explains the design and then presses his qualification. The decision was apparently very strange, and such as nothing could vindicate had it been executed; but Solomon saw that the only way to find the real mother was through the affection and tenderness she would inevitably show. He plainly foresaw that the true mother would rather relinquish her claim than see her child hewn in pieces before her eyes, while the pretender might well look on with indifference; so he ordered a trial that would put maternal love to its utmost proof, and it succeeded. Yet Clarke insists this was a proof of sound judgment, penetration, and knowledge of human nature rather than of extraordinary and supernatural wisdom, and he cites heathen parallels — the Emperor Claudius, who (as Suetonius reports) forced a woman to confess her son by ordering her to marry him; and Ariopharnes of Thrace, who found the true heir of the Cimmerian king by bidding each claimant shoot an arrow into the dead king's body, the real son alone refusing.

Against this stands the reading that the surrounding witnesses will voice — the puritan Matthew Henry, who observes that to find the true mother Solomon could not try which the child loved best and so tried which loved the child best, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, who calls the same stroke a touch of genius that Scripture itself names the wisdom of God. The debate is genuine: whether this was clever human insight into the heart, of a kind pagans have occasionally matched, or the divinely given discernment the chapter has been at pains to display. All agree on the mechanism — that the sword was never meant to fall, but to sift two hearts by the one test love cannot fake — and all agree it worked; they part only over how high to trace its source. Clarke keeps it on earth; the others read the wisdom of God in it. The stroke is one; the question is whose wisdom wielded it.
26:
"Then spake the woman whose the living child was unto the king, for her bowels yearned upon her son, and she said, O my lord, give her the living child, and in no wise slay it. But the other said, Let it be neither mine nor thine, but divide it." The witnesses read the verse as the instant the trap springs and the truth stands revealed. The reformed John Gill catches the urgency: the true mother spoke in haste and with great vehemence, lest the executioner should at once dispatch the child; her love could not endure even the threat of a moment's delay.

The verse turns on the contrast of the two responses, and the witnesses dwell on it. The one woman, hearing the dreadful sentence, is content — even eager — to have the child divided: let it be neither mine nor thine, but divide it. By this she betrays that she knows her own claim to be false, for she can watch the infant killed without a pang, caring only that her rival shall not have it. The other woman, at the same words, is undone; rather than see the child butchered she surrenders her whole claim to it, crying out that her adversary may have it living if only it be not slain. Her very willingness to lose the child proves the child is hers.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the two hearts exactly. She who knew the child was not her own stood upon a point of honour and was well content to have it divided; had she been the true mother she would never have forfeited her interest in it by agreeing so readily to so bloody a decision. But she who knew the child was her own, rather than see it slain, gave it up to her adversary — O my lord, give her the living child — choosing to see it hers no longer rather than not see it at all. By this tenderness it appeared she was no careless mother who could overlay a child, but the true parent, having compassion on the son of her womb. The witnesses land together on the deep principle the scene enacts: love will surrender its rights before it will see its beloved destroyed, and it is exactly this that distinguishes the mother from the pretender. The sword drew out what testimony never could — the difference between a heart that owns the child and a heart that merely contends for it. What no oath could establish, a mother's yearning proved in a single cry.
27:
"Then the king answered and said, Give her the living child, and in no wise slay it: she is the mother thereof." The witnesses read the verdict as the swift, sure conclusion the whole device was aimed at. The reformed John Gill marks the plain sentence by which Solomon awards the child to the woman who would sooner lose it than see it die, naming her, on the evidence of her own compassion, the mother.

The wisdom of the judgment lies in how completely it needs no further proof. The puritan Matthew Henry puts the king's reasoning in a phrase: the case is plain, what need of witnesses? All present had just seen, by her undissembled tenderness, which of the two was the mother; the sword had made the hidden heart visible, and the visible heart decided the cause. Where a whole court had stood helpless before two equal claims, a single instinctive cry had settled everything, and Solomon has only to read aloud the verdict love had already written.

Henry draws the verse toward its lessons. Let parents show their love to their children, he urges, above all by caring for their souls and, with a holy violence, snatching them as brands out of the burning; those are most likely to have the comfort of children who do their duty by them. And he finds a deeper figure in the false mother's willingness to divide: Satan pretends to the heart of man, but by this readiness to share he shows himself only a pretender, content to divide with God, whereas the rightful sovereign of the heart will have all or none. The witnesses let the verdict rest as both the vindication of true love and the display of a wisdom that could reach where evidence could not. The truth that two contradictory oaths had buried, the king brought to light by a stroke that forced love to speak; and the child is restored to the mother whose yearning had proved her right to him. Justice is done, not by weighing words that cancelled each other, but by discerning the heart that words concealed. So the God-given wisdom is vindicated in the very case that seemed to defy it.
28:
"And all Israel heard of the judgment which the king had judged; and they feared the king: for they saw that the wisdom of God was in him, to do judgment." The witnesses read the closing verse as the public seal set upon the whole chapter — the promised wisdom, displayed in one hard case, now owned by a whole nation. The reformed John Gill notes that the decision was divulged throughout the land, its fame spread everywhere, so that the private trial of two obscure women became a national testimony.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the effect plainly: this judicial decision convinced all the people that Solomon was endowed with divine wisdom for the administration of justice. The wesleyan Adam Clarke unfolds what their fear amounted to — they saw that they could not impose upon him, and were afraid to do the things that might bring them before his judgment-seat, perceiving that he was taught of God, judged impartially, and could not be deceived. Clarke adds the loose thread the narrative leaves untied: what became of the false mother we are not told, though justice, he remarks, certainly required that she be punished for her lies and fraud.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the fruit of it for Solomon's reign. This reputation would have a great influence on the ease of his government; the people feared him, highly reverenced him, durst not oppose him, and were afraid of doing an unjust thing, knowing that if it came before him he would certainly discover it. Henry crowns the account with the wisdom-sayings of Solomon's own book: this wisdom made his face to shine (Ecclesiastes 8:1), it strengthened him more than ten rulers in a city (Ecclesiastes 7:19), and it was better to him than weapons of war (Ecclesiastes 9:18), so that for it he was both feared and loved. The witnesses close where the chapter began, on the wisdom of God. What was asked at Gibeon in the humility of a little child, and given in a dream, is here seen working in the open, drawing truth out of a sealed case and awe out of a whole people; and the phrase the wisdom of God was in him points beyond Solomon to the greater Son of David, in whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, and who shall judge the secrets of men. The petition of the dream has become the settled character of the throne.
